items: All molecular and connectomic predictors are derived from healthy cohorts; disease- and age-specific alterations in these features are not modeled, potentially biasing inference about pathophysiological mechanisms.

Multicollinearity among molecular predictors (e.g., gene PC1, myelination, metabolism) complicates attribution in linear/dominance analyses; PC1 reductions may obscure receptor- or gene-specific effects.
The neighbor abnormality metric divides by degree while multiplying by SC weights; without normalization by the sum of weights, weighted averaging may be biased by degree heterogeneity.
Centrality definitions on weighted graphs can be sensitive to weight-to-length transformations; precise choices and robustness are not fully detailed.
Analyses are restricted to 68 DK parcels, precluding subcortex/cerebellum and limiting spatial resolution; receptor/transcriptomic maps may lose informative variance at this scale.
The ENIGMA-derived effect sizes integrate heterogeneous cohorts (medication, comorbidities, chronicity), but potential systematic confounds are not explicitly modeled.
Linear models may underfit non-linear or interaction effects among predictors; mediation and moderation between molecular vulnerability and network exposure are not formally tested.
Structural connectivity is from a single, modest sample (n=70) and a single tractography/reconstruction approach; generalization across connectome datasets is not shown.
Main-text emphasis is on in-sample adjusted R²; cross-validated performance and uncertainty (e.g., confidence/credible intervals) could be more prominent in the main figures.
Some choices (e.g., parameterization of centrality on weighted networks, normalization in exposure computations) are deferred to Methods and could benefit from added precision in the main text.

Mechanistic network-diffusion/convolution models of pathology spread (e.g., in AD) show sensitivity to graph operators and kernels; a brief contrast would contextualize the exposure findings and suggest extensions.
Recent multi-level association frameworks that identify dense multimodal subnetworks (e.g., MOAT) could provide complementary inference beyond per-region regressions.
“In-sensor adaptive machine learning” and “standalone” claims appear overstated: most ML processing is performed in the cloud; the on-patch MCU is not executing the CNN or on-device adaptation.
Potential safety and durability risks of Ag nanowires in hydrogels (e.g., ion release, long-term skin exposure) are not comprehensively characterized beyond short-term cell viability.
Limited discussion of antenna/body-channel detuning under real use, coexistence interference, and BLE link robustness under movement and sweat.

Small human cohort (training on 5 subjects, testing on 2), all apparently healthy; no clinical population data; limited assessment of day-to-day variability, re-attachment, or sensor placement shifts.
HR/RR validation is limited and includes a likely labeling error in example values (reported HR ≈ 19.2 bpm vs RR ≈ 50.4/min, which is physiologically implausible for HR).
Limited longitudinal wear testing (hours–days), sweat/water exposure studies, hydration/dehydration cycles of hydrogel, and motion artifacts under real ambulatory conditions.

Claims of robustness to motion artifacts are not benchmarked against strong, recent flexible electrode baselines; SNR/PSD comparisons to a “commercial device” lack precise specification and standardized metrics.
Insufficient detail on the “2D-like sequential feature extractor” (exact tensorization procedure, architecture specifics, parameter counts), and on triplet loss configuration and sampling strategy.
The term “adaptive” is used to describe generalization to new subjects without explicit on-device or online adaptation; this can be misleading.
Return loss description (“< 10 dB”) is ambiguous; conventionally S11 < −10 dB indicates acceptable matching; signs/units should be explicit.

No systematic comparison to sequence models that explicitly handle temporal dependencies (e.g., LSTM/GRU, TCNs), or to simpler feature-based classifiers; no ablation of modality contributions (sEMG vs ACC vs fusion).
Limited discussion of on-device ML or model compression/pruning to enable true in-sensor inference compared to emerging wearable edge-AI platforms (e.g., BioGAP-Ultra).
Minimal connection to recent large-scale SSL/pretraining for wearables that could improve generalization (e.g., strategies akin to day-long context modeling with learned masking).
The electrochemical dissolution of gold generates AuCl4−; local ocular safety, cumulative gold ion exposure, and clearance are not quantified or discussed in depth.

Cross-sensitivities of the strain sensor to temperature, hydration, ocular biomechanics (e.g., corneal thickness, stiffness), and tear osmolarity are only partially addressed; TCR exists but impact on IOP accuracy is not modeled.
Long-term mechanical and electrical stability of the sensor/DDS/packaging in the tear environment (salt, proteins, lipids) and under blink-induced shear has not been demonstrated.
Oxygen permeability (Dk/t) and tear exchange in the presence of embedded films/passivation layers are not reported; chronic hypoxia risk remains unaddressed.
In vivo sample sizes, variance, and statistical testing details (n, error bars, blinding) are inconsistently reported; outcomes such as “22% IOP reduction” would benefit from full statistics.
Agreement with tonometry (R² = 0.88; LoA ≈ ±3 mmHg) is promising but not benchmarked against existing devices (e.g., Triggerfish, implantables); drift over days/weeks was not quantified.

Pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics of timolol from the DDS (ocular tissue levels, aqueous humor concentrations, systemic exposure) are not measured; comparison with eye drops focuses on outcomes but lacks PK support.
Weekly-type DDS is proposed, but in vivo data appear limited to daily/every-other-day use; multi-dose reliability and inter-dose crosstalk are not fully explored on-eye.

Wireless power/communication and ASIC details are deferred to prior work, limiting reproducibility; basic specs (frequency, modulation, telemetry rate, readout resolution, noise, rectifier efficiency) are missing here.

Some fabrication parameters critical to reproducibility (e.g., exact Au shell thickness distribution, residual Ag quantification, PEDOT:PSS formulation and crosslinking details) are not fully specified.

A systematic comparison to prior IOP-sensing lenses (Triggerfish, graphene/AgNW/silicon sensors) in a performance table is missing, making it hard to contextualize advances in sensitivity, transparency, drift, and clinical agreement.
Prior art on on-eye drug delivery (e.g., hydrogel-lens reservoirs, micro-reservoir MEMS, osmotic lenses) is not comprehensively discussed, especially safety/PK learnings relevant to this DDS.
Ambiguity of MS/MS-based attribution (e.g., isomers/stereoisomers, shared substructures) is inherent; reliance on cosine similarity without explicit FDR control can inflate false positives.
Propagation of matches up the taxonomy without specificity metrics can be misleading when few samples or clades dominate coverage.
Instrumental heterogeneity (ion mode, collision energy, adducts) and media/background signals may affect match reliability; harmonization strategies are not described in depth.
Lacks quantitative benchmarking of precision/recall, false discovery rates (via decoys or permutation), or comparisons to MASST or fragment-centric alternatives for taxonomic inference.
Limited analysis of coverage bias (uneven representation of taxa/strains) and its effect on producer inference or apparent ubiquity.
Human-cell-line filtering is helpful but insufficient to rule out host origin; inclusion of broader host tissues or orthogonal validation (e.g., isotope tracing, genomic/BGC evidence) is not shown.
Search parameter defaults (m/z tolerance, fragment tolerances, minimum matched peaks, score thresholds) and their impact on sensitivity/specificity are not quantitatively explored.
The analog search setting is mentioned but not characterized for how often it yields biologically plausible versus spurious lineage attributions.
Limited discussion of fragment-centric taxonomy inference (e.g., MSFragDB/MFN) or recent ML-based spectral alignment tools that might complement or benchmark the approach.
Broader placement relative to targeted microbiome-metabolome inference frameworks is brief; could connect to genome mining resources (MIBiG/antiSMASH) for orthogonal producer validation.
